what_it_does: This file is a front-end handoff document that specifies how to build the authoring UX for the cognition layer (roles, rules, structured outputs, modes/casts). It documents the already-built backend endpoints, request/response data shapes, surfaces to extend or add, governance laws, and backend file locations.
observations: The backend is described as built and verified (42 checks in tests/authoring_acceptance.py) on the concurrent-cognition branch, not yet merged.
It lists read endpoints (/api/cognition_info, /api/stream) and write endpoints for roles (propose, edit, delete) and rules (validate, dry_run, attach, detach), plus dry-run and preview endpoints.
All writes follow a propose-not-apply governance loop: role/propose or role/edit surfaces an item, the operator approves via /api/resolve, and /api/apply writes the file.
Rule conditions are a closed data-AST (RULE_OPS) with deterministic ops only; there is no now, random, call, or IO op.
There are exactly five routing destination kinds: inject, chain, address, surface, lane; resolve/approve/dispatch are explicitly forbidden.
RoleFieldSpec uses a closed set of field types (str, int, float, bool, list[str], list[int]) and output_fields define a Pydantic schema.

The FE should reuse existing surfaces: Palette, Inspector, NodeConfigForm, CognitionView, Inbox, and the cognition.* SSE fold; net-new parts are the rule-builder, schema editor, role form, and test/preview panel.
Eight numbered laws govern the UI: reflects-never-owns, propose-not-apply, closed renderable grammar, lead-only floor, run:// addressing, author from registry, fail loud, and protected roles.
Backend locations are cross-referenced: runtime/authoring.py, runtime/suite.py, runtime/bridge.py, contracts/cognition_info.py, runtime/rules.py, runtime/roles.py, ops/cli/capabilities.py, and tests.
standouts: The entire authoring backend is already built and acceptance-tested; the front-end's job is UX only.
Authoring cognition is framed as composing the same node-graph substrate the app already renders generically.
The governance loop requires operator approval before any role file is written; the UI must never auto-apply.
The rule grammar is closed, depth-capped, and renderable as an edge badge, with a hard floor excluding resolve/approve/dispatch destinations.
conventions: Uses the object_info/ui_info generic renderer pattern from the existing canvas.
Presents endpoint contracts in request/response tables with JSONc examples.
Maps each UX affordance to an existing surface to extend and the backend endpoint it drives.
Enumerates explicit numbered laws the UI must honor.
Provides a backend cross-reference section for tracing implementations.
concerns: References external documents and section codes (C7.4/C7.5, C1.3, C9.2, 06-rendering.md, AGENTS.md) without defining them locally.
The opening framing 'a separate session (this one) built the entire authoring BACKEND' is slightly self-referential and could confuse which session the reader is in.
The phrase 'the claude -p floor is lead-only' is colloquial and may be opaque without context.
The document asserts the FE is 'just UX,' but the rule-builder and schema editor appear to be substantial new interactive components.
Protected-role handling is mentioned but not detailed in UI terms.
notes: A front-end handoff that tells the FE session how to wire the already-built cognition authoring backend into existing palette/inspector/inbox surfaces while obeying a propose-approve-apply governance loop and a closed, deterministic rule grammar.
questions: What are the exact visual/interaction specs for the rule-builder AST composer and the schema editor?
Where and how is the directory-scan source node intended to be implemented, since only the structured-output half is covered here?
What do the section codes C7.4, C7.5, C1.3, and C9.2 refer to in the referenced documents?
How should the UI visually indicate and handle protected roles when edit/delete returns {protected: true}?
What is the intended merge timeline or dependency order for the concurrent-cognition branch?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file states it is a handoff for the front-end authoring surface and then documents the backend contract, data shapes, reusable surfaces, and laws needed to implement that surface.
apparent_themes: Reflects-never-owns: backend is the source of truth
Reuse existing generic renderer and inspector patterns
Fail loud and enforce protected boundaries
intention_signals: Enable a front-end developer to build the cognition authoring surface by reusing existing canvas UI patterns, without modifying backend plumbing, while ensuring every mutation surfaces for operator approval and rules remain deterministic and visually legible.
novelty: Treats authoring cognition as composing the same node graph the runtime uses, and mandates a closed, depth-capped, renderable data-AST for rules instead of free-form code or expressions.
contribution: Provides a complete contract between the already-implemented cognition authoring backend and the front-end, covering endpoint semantics, request/response shapes, closed rule grammar, destination kinds, governance loop, reusable UI surfaces, and enforceable FE laws.
summary_for_embedding: A front-end handoff document specifying the API contract, data shapes, reusable UI surfaces, and governance laws for authoring cognition roles and rules in a node-graph runtime.
intention_for_embedding: To guide the front-end implementation of the cognition authoring surface while ensuring it reuses existing renderer patterns and respects the propose-approve-apply governance model.
